title: Sattu and cooling summer drinks
excerpt: Every summer, people reach for iced water or cold drinks to escape the heat. Ayurveda offers something older and more effective: the panaka and sattu tradition, a family of cooling preparations that strengthen the body while genuinely bringing down internal heat.
body_html:
the summer problem ayurveda names precisely

When the temperature climbs in May and June, you notice something beyond simple sweating. Your energy drops by afternoon. Appetite shrinks. A glass of iced water gives a brief lift, then the fatigue returns. Ayurveda identifies this state as Grishma Ritu daurbalya, the weakening that summer brings, and it traces the cause clearly: the season's intense heat aggravates Pitta dosha while simultaneously depleting Ojas, the body's foundational vitality.

The classical texts describe summer as a time when the sun's rays draw moisture upward, drying out both the earth and the human body (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Agni, the digestive fire, becomes erratic: weakened for heavy foods, yet overstimulated at the surface. The Sushruta Samhita notes that people in summer should favour light, liquid, and cooling food above all else (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 20). This is not a counsel of rest but a practical reorientation of what you eat and drink.

a cold drink relieves heat for fifteen minutes, then leaves them more sluggish than before

sattu and panaka: what the texts actually recommend

Sattu is roasted gram flour, usually made from barley or Bengal gram, ground fine and mixed with water, a pinch of rock salt, a squeeze of lime, and sometimes a little roasted cumin. The Bhavaprakasha describes sattu as both tarpana (nourishing, satisfying) and sheetala (cooling), and notes that it alleviates thirst and replenishes the body after exertion (Bhavaprakasha, Dhanyavarga). It is not a snack. It is a considered formulation: the roasting process makes the gram light and easy to digest, the salt replaces what sweat takes, and the lime aids absorption.

Panaka is a broader category of sweetened, spiced cooling drinks described in detail in the Sushruta Samhita (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsa Sthana). The most familiar version is jal jeera, water infused with roasted cumin, black salt, lime, and a little mint. Another is the rose-water or dried-fruit sharbat found at dargahs and temples across North India. Aam panna, raw mango concentrate with jeera and rock salt, sits squarely in this tradition. What these drinks share is a set of properties the texts prize together: they are cooling but not refrigerant, lightly spiced to keep digestion active, and never made icy.

The aversion to ice is worth understanding rather than dismissing. Ayurveda holds that very cold food and drink temporarily dampen Agni. In summer, when Agni is already weakened, this suppression means even light food is not digested cleanly. The result is the paradox many people notice: a cold drink relieves heat for fifteen minutes, then leaves them more sluggish than before. The classical preference is for drinks at room temperature or only slightly cool, naturally chilled by earthen pots (matka water), which cool through evaporation without shocking the digestive system (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

simple daily habits through the hot months

The practical advice from these texts is easy to work into a normal day. In the morning, a glass of sattu mixed in water with a small pinch of rock salt and lime makes a light, sustaining breakfast. It is high in protein, digests without burdening a heat-tired system, and keeps energy steady through the first half of the day. For people doing physical work in the sun, the texts' praise of sattu as a tarpana makes particular sense.

Through the afternoon, cooled herbal water is more useful than plain chilled water. Coriander seeds steeped overnight in a covered vessel, then strained and drunk in the afternoon, are a common household remedy for Pitta-related heat, heartburn, and irritability. Fennel similarly has a reputation in the Bhavaprakasha as a digestive coolant. Both herbs keep digestion moving while reducing internal heat, which is the combination summer actually requires.

Food choices matter as well. The Caraka Samhita advises avoiding pungent, sour, and salty tastes in excess during Grishma, and favouring sweet and bitter tastes, which in practical terms means more rice, moong dal, cucumber, bottle gourd, and fresh coconut water, and less of the fried, fermented, and heavily spiced foods that feel tempting but aggravate Pitta. Evening meals should be light. Sleeping in a cool, ventilated room and avoiding overexertion in the midday heat are mentioned alongside diet as part of the seasonal regimen (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

when to see a doctor

These suggestions are general information rooted in classical Ayurvedic tradition. They are not a diagnosis and are not a substitute for medical care. If you or someone in your family shows signs of heatstroke, such as confusion, very high body temperature, stopping sweating despite intense heat, rapid heartbeat, or loss of consciousness, seek emergency medical attention immediately. Similarly, if symptoms such as severe dizziness, fainting, persistent vomiting, or extreme weakness appear after heat exposure, see a doctor without delay. People with kidney disease, diabetes, or blood pressure conditions should consult their physician before making significant dietary changes. A qualified Ayurvedic vaidya can advise on preparations and doses suited to your individual constitution.

Try This Today

  • Morning sattu glass: Mix 2 tablespoons of roasted gram sattu in a glass of water. Add a pinch of rock salt and a squeeze of lime. Stir well and drink at room temperature. Best on an empty or light stomach.
  • Overnight coriander water: Soak a teaspoon of whole coriander seeds in a covered glass of water overnight. Strain and drink in the afternoon. Useful for reducing afternoon heat, mild heartburn, and irritability.
  • Jal jeera at home: Dry-roast a teaspoon of cumin seeds until fragrant. Grind coarsely. Mix with water, a pinch of black salt, a pinch of dried mint, and lime juice. Drink at room temperature or from a matka.
  • Switch to a matka: Replace your refrigerator water bottle with a terracotta matka or surahi for at least the summer months. Evaporative cooling keeps water naturally cool without chilling it to the point of dampening digestion.
  • Fennel after meals: Chew a small pinch of fennel seeds after lunch and dinner in summer. The Bhavaprakasha notes fennel's cooling, digestive properties: a low-effort habit that reduces post-meal heat and bloating.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Cumin (जीरा)

Dry-roasted and used in jal jeera and panaka; supports digestion while reducing internal heat

Coriander (धनिया)

Steeped overnight in water; a classical cooling remedy for afternoon Pitta heat and mild heartburn

Fennel (सौंफ)

Chewed after meals in summer; Bhavaprakasha notes its cooling and digestive properties
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Every summer, people reach for iced water or cold drinks to escape the heat. Ayurveda offers something older and more effective: the panaka and sattu tradition, a family of cooling preparations that strengthen the body while genuinely bringing down internal heat.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Sattu (roasted gram flour drink) is a classical Ayurvedic tarpana: cooling, protein-rich, and easy to digest in summer heat
Panaka-style drinks like jal jeera and aam panna cool without icing, keeping digestion active while reducing Pitta
Very cold drinks temporarily dampen digestive fire; matka water and room-temperature herbal drinks are the classical preference

glossary_terms.definition: The summer season in Ayurveda's six-season calendar, typically mid-April to mid-June, characterised by intense solar heat that depletes bodily moisture and weakens digestion
The dosha governing heat, metabolism, and transformation; aggravated by summer and by spicy, sour, and fried foods
The subtle essence of all seven bodily tissues; the foundation of immunity and vitality, depleted by excessive heat, stress, and poor sleep
Nourishing and satisfying to the tissues; a quality the texts assign to sattu, meaning it replenishes rather than merely quenches
Cooling in quality; one of the key properties Ayurveda looks for in summer foods and drinks
A classical category of sweetened or spiced cooling drinks; the family of preparations that includes jal jeera, sharbat, and aam panna

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ritucharya (seasonal regimen for Grishma)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ritucharya (Grishma Ritu diet and regimen)
Bhavaprakasha, Dhanyavarga, properties of sattu and cooling grains
Sushruta Samhita, Ritucharya sections (panaka and cooling drinks for summer)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Grishma Ritu" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Grishma Ritu is defined as: The summer season in Ayurveda's six-season calendar, typically mid-April to mid-June, characterised by intense solar heat that depletes bodily moisture and weakens digestion

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to morning sattu glass. Mix 2 tablespoons of roasted gram sattu in a glass of water. Add a pinch of rock salt and a squeeze of lime. Stir well and drink at room temperature. Best on an empty or light stomach.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Cumin for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Cumin is highly regarded. Dry-roasted and used in jal jeera and panaka; supports digestion while reducing internal heat
